narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's model predicted a 59.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. Your profile shows some real strengths, like an impressive LSAT score of 40—well above the typical student's 36.7—and a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.3, compared to the average 3.21. You're from an upper-middle income family, identify as male and White, and plan to attend full-time, all of which align closely with the majority of students in the dataset.

However, your school decile ranks stood out as major concerns: you placed in the 9th decile for Year 1 grades and again in Year 3, meaning your performance was in the lower tier compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack ranks around the 5th or 6th decile. The model weighed your Year 3 decile rank as the single most influential factor, pushing its prediction toward failure more than anything else. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile rank, which also strongly nudged the odds toward a fail prediction.

Your family income bracket came next in influence, contributing in a way that heightened the risk, followed closely by your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA, both of which unfortunately added to the predicted likelihood of struggling on the bar. Your racial background had a modest push toward failure as well, while your gender as male provided a slight counterbalance, tilting things a tiny bit toward success—though it wasn't enough to outweigh the rest. Your full-time status had virtually no impact either way.

Overall, these academic decile ranks painted a picture of inconsistent performance that overshadowed your test scores and background, setting you apart from typical students who balance stronger school rankings with similar credentials. To improve your future chances, focus on building consistent study habits and academic performance, much like what got you that strong LSAT—perhaps through targeted prep courses or tutoring to mimic the trajectories of students who pass the bar. With time, strengthening those early-grade weaknesses could flip the model's view and open doors at other schools. Keep pushing; your potential is clear in those standout scores.
